content:
- Olympus SaaS Check-in - Monthly (Office Hours with Ramki Gaddipati)

II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call:
- This meeting was a monthly check-in for the Olympus SaaS initiative, focusing on refining the Olympus product and entity modeling, especially around the new “estate” construct, and clarifying the relationships between tenants, SREs, publishers, and estates. The objective was to deliberate on how the model supports multiple organizational scenarios (e.g., large enterprises acting as tenants, publishers, SREs). Ramki guided participants through the rationale, emphasizing real-world implications for onboarding varied customers and partners, technical boundary conditions, and how Olympus’ resource packaging (estate, Watch, Hub, Cipher) can create differentiated value. The discussion included deep dives into what it means for software or organizations to be “resident” versus “citizen,” organizational hierarchies within estates, and operationalizing these concepts for productization and customer communication. There was an ongoing effort to clarify terminology, entity boundaries, and practical implementation strategies, with Ramki providing core philosophical as well as pragmatic guidance.

IV. Key Takeaways from the Call:

- Technical Model Clarified: Olympus system to treat estates as collections of resources, not merely organizational collections (“estate is a resource to manage”).
- Residency Over Citizenship: Shifted from “citizen” to “resident” paradigm for software acceptance in Olympus for greater flexibility and inclusivity (“we only need residency”).
- Flexible Organizational Relationships: Tenants may act as tenants, publishers, or SREs independently or in combination; estate is optional and not every tenant needs one.
- Estate Value Proposition: Estates package resources, publishers, customer orgs, and SRE orgs, enabling scenario sales to large enterprises (e.g., HDFC can bring all roles).
- Publisher/SRE Independence: Publisher and SRE orgs can be associated with estates or exist independently—model supports both.
- Operational Flexibility: SRE responsibility can span sites, products, or both; operational practices (like Olympus Watch) included in estate, but Watch as a stand-alone product is not yet viable.
- High Adoption Commitment: Olympus products (estate, Hub, Cipher, Watch) require significant customer commitment; not likely to be low-friction/product-led sales.
- Open Modeling Questions Remain: Further detailed technical/operational design needed, e.g., how customer orgs and estates relate, how default associations are handled.

V. Ramki's Insights:

1. Entity Model Philosophy & Clarity:
- Ramki emphasized the importance of clear entity demarcation in Olympus, with estates serving as resource containers, separating business and technical management. He challenged the historical reliance on “citizenship” for entities and advocated a paradigm change to the more practical “residency,” signaling a non-restrictive stance: Olympus should be an open, flexible substrate for a variety of stakeholders.

2. Onboarder Versatility & Optionality:
- With conviction, Ramki asserted that tenants should be able to exist as tenants, publishers, and/or SREs, independently or in combination, according to business needs. He opposed unnecessary coupling (e.g., requiring everyone to have an estate) and cautioned against forcing default constructs unless justified. His tone was guiding, sometimes corrective—questioning strict associations and seeking the minimal, most flexible viable model.

3. Estate as a Business Enabler:
- He articulated, with enthusiasm and granularity, the differentiated value of the estate construct for large enterprises: enabling full-spectrum participation (owning infra, publishing software, running their own operations), enhanced control over software acceptance, security, and operational practices—akin to how app stores vet apps: “Insert all your vendors to publish to Olympus so that it just maintains the sanity...”. Estate is positioned as a managed, policy-driven boundary, “packaging” the necessary capabilities.

4. Distinction between Operational and Business Roles:
- Ramki repeatedly distinguished operational (SRE) versus business (world owner) responsibilities, advocating for explicit, model-based representation of these roles. He explained with context-rich scenarios why, for instance, SRE orgs must not be restricted to Zeta, and world owners can be commercial, not just technical.

5. On Productization & Go-to-market:
- Ramki was candid about the challenges of packaging and selling Olympus components. He recognized the high commitment required for full adoption (“not a low-friction play”, “cost of adoption of estate is a very, very big cost”). He advised careful consideration of customer journeys and adoption barriers, particularly for Olympus Watch, and was skeptical—but open—about finding a feasible path to market for certain standalone offerings.

6. Instructions for Further Model Refinement:
- Throughout, Ramki instructed the team to keep the entity model as simple and minimal as possible, cautioned against implicit associations, and encouraged iterative, policy-driven layering for new constructs like estate. His approach emphasized adaptability, modularity, and clarity in both product and process.

VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items:

1. Action: Prepare/refine narrative diagrams and documentation to clarify Olympus’ current and new entity relationships (estate, tenants, SRE orgs, etc.).
- Assigned To: General team (in context, likely Mohit and Eswar)
- Instructions: Ramki wants diagrams and narratives to capture both the solid-line (“present state”) and dotted-line (“new/optional associations”) relationships. Diagrams should focus on clarity—emphasizing resource-centric views and optional entity boundaries.
- Timeline: As soon as possible, before next major review/demo (“block time over the weekend”).

2. Action: Prepare a document outlining (for review) the model of estate, tenant org, SRE org, publisher org—their associations, and options for onboarding use cases.
- Assigned To: Eswar Kumar Perabathini (Mohit to initiate, Eswar to present/correct course)
- Instructions: Summarize how Olympus system allows for flexible combinations of tenants, estates, publishers, and SREs; clarify “residency” model; provide illustrative customer flows (e.g., HDFC as estate/tenant/publisher/SRE).
- Timeline: Before the next Olympus estate-focused session (implied: within a week, before Optum/other demos).

3. Action: Evaluate and scope “Watch” as a standalone, independently salable product versus as part of an estate.
- Assigned To: Mohit Srivastava (to scope, with further feedback from Ramki)
- Instructions: Ramki wants a critical look at feasibility and adoption cost for Watch as a standalone product, noting potential adoption challenges, comparing to Datadog, and ensuring the customer journey is practical before investing in standalone packaging.
- Timeline: No strict deadline, but to be reviewed ahead of any narratives for next proposals/customer discussions.

VII. Jira Ticket:

Based on action items:

- Scope: Prepare comprehensive Olympus entity model/narrative diagrams and supporting doc—solid and dotted relationships, resource-centric perspectives.
- Assignee: Eswar Kumar Perabathini (with input from Mohit Srivastava)
- Justification: Critical for engineering and GTM alignment; clear documentation and diagrams of the estate-centered entity model underpin further productization efforts and resolve recurring confusion in onboarding and product packaging.

- Other action items (Watch standalone scoping) do not rise to the level of a Jira ticket at this point, as per Ramki's explicit uncertainty about their immediate feasibility and his directive to “scope before proceeding.”

VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions:

- Assess if the “residency” model necessitates any material change to onboarding flows or database schemas for Olympus products.
- For the entity diagrams—explicitly call out optional versus required relationships, to highlight the flexibility Ramki emphasized.
- Prepare customer-facing narrative examples (as suggested for HDFC) in the documentation to improve field and demo readiness.
- In next session, clarify if/how estate-level “policy” management will be layered without encumbering tenants or defaulting associations unnecessarily.
- Review and agree on terminology (estate, residency, resource ownership) to ensure all internal and external communications are aligned.

attendees: Venkata Praveen Irrinki (and mentions of Ajay
Kamlesh—likely not present for main discussion)
